set.instructions:
Lie on your back with the back of your legs on a foam roller, just above the ankle

Place your arms flat by your side

Posteriorly tilt your pelvis and brace your core, then raise your two legs (keeping them relaxed) as high as possible without the knee bending

Start the movement by engaging your core and lowering one of your legs until you touch the foam roller, maintaining the position with the other leg

Pause slightly and then return to the start position in a controlled manner, keeping constant tension in your core

Keep a neutral spine throughout

Anchor a band around the middle of a chin-up bar

Stand on a step, bench, etc, and place one foot in the band. Grasp a chin-up bar with shoulder-width apart position so your palms are facing you

Engage your core and ensure you have a neutral spine

Begin by carefully stepping forward off the step until you’re hanging with your arms straight and holding your full bodyweight.

Leg with the band should be straight down. Either wrap your free foot around your other foot or bend the leg behind you to 90-degrees. Keep a neutral spine for the entire movement

Then depress your shoulder blades down and pull yourself up towards the bar, driving your elbows down in the process

Pull up until your chin clears the bar, keeping your eyes and head level for the entire move

Pause at the top and then return to the start position in a slow controlled manner, ensuring constant tension in your lats, core, and upper back

Anchor a band around the middle of a pull-up bar

Stand on a step, bench, etc, and place one foot in the band. Grasp a pull up bar with an overhand grip and place them wider than shoulder-width apart

Engage your core and ensure you have a neutral spine

Begin by carefully stepping forward off the step until you’re hanging with your arms straight and holding your full bodyweight.

Leg with the band should be straight down. Either wrap your free foot around your other foot or bend the leg behind you to 90-degrees. Keep a neutral spine for the entire movement

Then depress your shoulder blades down and pull yourself up towards the bar, driving your elbows down in the process

Pull up until your chin clears the bar, keeping your eyes and head level for the entire move

Pause at the top and then return to the start position in a slow controlled manner, ensuring constant tension in your lats, core, and upper back

Position a band under both feet and grasp the ends with your hands with a neutral grip, palms facing towards the midline of your body

Stand tall with your arms straight by your side

A neutral spine should be maintained for the entire movement and eyes focused looking forward

Start by bracing your core and then hinging at the hips until your torso is as parallel to the floor as your mobility allows

Then retract your shoulder blades while making sure to keep your shoulders depressed down. Then quickly bring both hands up towards your torso in a controlled manner by rowing your elbows to your side. Squeeze your shoulder blades together at this point

Pulling motion should be initiated from your lats and middle back, rather than just your arms

Pause and then reverse the movement by returning to the start position in a slow controlled manner, making sure to keep constant tension in your core, lats, and upper back while maintaining a neutral spine

Position a band under both feet and grasp with both hands. Place your arms straight by your side with your palms facing forwards

Stand with your feet shoulder-width apart and head looking forward

Brace your core and make sure you have a neutral spine. Your arms shouldn’t be locked out

With your arms locked in by your side, begin the movement by bending at the elbows and curling the band up in a controlled fashion

Curl the band as high as you can while maintaining constant tension on your biceps and keeping your upper arms fixed by your side

Be careful not to shrug your shoulders up as you curl the band, and be sure to avoid any swinging to create momentum

Pause when you reach this point and then return to the start position in a slow controlled manner, ensuring constant tension on your biceps and core

Stop just short of locking out your arms and repeat

Anchor a band and set it up in a prone position on the floor with the band around one leg

Your legs should be straight and there should be no slack in the band

Posteriorly tilt your pelvis and engage your core, ensuring you have a neutral spine

Begin by pulling your heel in towards your bum in a controlled manner and squeeze your hamstring. Movement should come solely from bending your knee

When you reach the end of your range of motion, pause, and then return to the start position in a slow controlled manner, ensuring constant tension in your hamstring

If you find that you can’t keep your spine neutral, you can place a pad/mat underneath your stomach to help with your position

Repeat

In a split stance, position a band under both feet and grasp the middle of the band with one hand in a neutral grip, palm facing towards the midline of your body

Stand tall with your arm straight by your side

A neutral spine should be maintained for the entire movement and eyes focused looking forward

Start by bracing your core and then hinging at the hips until your torso is as parallel to the floor as your mobility allows

Then retract your shoulder blade while making sure to keep your shoulders depressed down. Then quickly pull the band up towards your torso in a controlled manner by rowing your elbow to your side. Squeeze your shoulder blades together at this point

Pulling motion should be initiated from your lat and middle back, rather than just your arm

Pause and then reverse the movement by returning to the start position in a slow controlled manner, making sure to keep constant tension in your core, lat, and upper back while maintaining a neutral spine

Position a rope just above knee height on a cable pulley

Grab the rope between your legs and step forward so your back is to the machine. Brace yourself and stand with your feet hip-width apart

Stand in an upright position with a neutral spine and your knees in line with your toes. Shoulders depressed down and head looking forward

From here, while bracing your core, retract your shoulder blades and engage your lats (imagine crushing two oranges between your armpits)

Begin the movement by hinging at the hips and letting the rope get pulled through your legs in a slow controlled manner. Push your hips back as far as your mobility allows (imagine pushing a door closed with your bum)

Your hands should travel as high up your legs as possible while moving through smoothly and keeping the knees soft

Your torso should lower towards the floor as your hips go backward, ensuring you maintain a neutral spine

When you reach the end of your range of motion, pause and then return to the start position by driving the hips forward and pulling the rope back through, squeezing your glutes in the process

Maintain constant tension through the core, glutes, hamstrings, and upper back

Lay on the floor on your back with your feet flat together along your centre line

Position a band around your knees. Can place above or below, find what position gives you the best glute activation

Get into a b-stance by moving one leg forward, placing your heel just in front of the other foot

Head should be neutral or chin tucked (a yoga block or Bosu ball can help prop your head up)

Arms can either be bent by your side with clenched fists or flat by your side

Start by posteriorly tilting your pelvis and bracing your core, then extend and drive your hips up by pushing through the heel of the foot closest to your body

The majority of your weight should be on the leg closest to you, the extended leg is purely for balance and stability

Make sure to keep your ribs down as this will help prevent spinal extension (lower back curving up) and keep your spine stable

When you reach the end of your range of motion, pause and squeeze your glute at the top. Return to the start position by reversing the movement in a slow controlled manner, keeping constant tension in the glute and core

Set up a squat rack so the barbell sits at chest height

Grasp the bar just outside shoulder width and step under the middle of the bar with both feet so it’s positioned on the upper back and shoulders, squeezing your shoulder blades together and lifting the elbows up to create a “shelf” for the bar to sit on. This position is dependent on the mobility of the upper body, so find what is comfortable for you

Next, stand up so the barbell comes off the rack and then carefully step far enough back from the rack

Foot position: Start with feet shoulder-width apart and slightly turned out. Some people will prefer a wider stance, or one with the feet turned out even more or with the toes pointed forward. Find what’s comfortable

Once the foot position is set, screw the feet into the floor. This will help you create torque as well as grip the floor. Your bodyweight should be centered over the middle of your foot to ensure balance

Stand in an upright position with a neutral spine, with your knees in line with your toes

Start the movement by bracing your core and then bending simultaneously at both the hips and knees, slowly lowering yourself in a controlled manner to the end of your range of motion. A good marker is your thigh parallel to the ground. Everyone is different here. Find a depth that allows you to maintain the correct form and a neutral spine

Keep your knees in line with your toes on the descent and your torso as upright as possible

When you reach the bottom of the squat pause for 1-2 seconds and then return to standing by pushing through both feet and driving up from the bottom, ensuring constant tension in your core throughout

Make sure to drive your knees out on the way up and keep your chest up

The barbell should travel up and down over the middle of the foot in a vertical line

Pause and repeat

Grasp a pull-up bar with an overhand grip and place them inside shoulder width. Stand on a step, bench, etc if needed

Engage your core and ensure you have a neutral spine

If elevated up, begin by carefully stepping forward off the step until you’re hanging with your arms straight and holding your full bodyweight

Either place your legs straight down and feet slightly in front or hook them behind you. Keep a neutral spine for the entire movement

Then depress your shoulder blades down and pull yourself up towards the bar, driving your elbows down in the process

Pull up until your chin clears the bar, keeping your eyes and head level for the entire move

Pause at the top and then return to the start position in a slow controlled manner, ensuring constant tension in your lats, core, and upper back

Face a box or step with feet shoulder-width apart and facing forward (choose a height that matches your ability)

Carefully pick up or have a partner hand you dumbbell in each hand and have arms by your side

Place one foot flat on the step and have your head facing forward, making sure you have a neutral spine

Begin by bracing your core and then stepping up by pushing through the box with the front leg and placing the trail leg on the box

There should be no assistance from the trail leg as the front leg should be solely responsible for the movement up

Spine should remain neutral throughout and your torso upright

From here, reverse the movement by stepping back with the trail leg and descending in a slow controlled manner until your reach the floor, making sure to keep constant tension in your glute, quad and core

To set up, begin with a curl or clean the dumbbells into the front rack position with the weights resting on your shoulders

Foot position: Start with feet shoulder-width apart and slightly turned out. Some people will prefer a wider stance, or one with the feet turned out even more or with the toes pointed forward. Find what’s comfortable

Once foot position is set, screw the feet into the floor. This will help you create torque as well as grip the floor. Your bodyweight should be centred over the middle of your foot to ensure balance

Stand in an upright position with a neutral spine, with your knees in line with your toes

Bring your hands out in front to help with balance and have your eyes focused forward

Start the movement by bracing your core and then bending simultaneously at both the hips and knees, slowly lowering yourself in a controlled manner to the end of your range of motion. A good marker is your thigh parallel to the ground. Everyone is different here. Find a depth that allows you to maintain correct form and a neutral spine

Keep your knees in line with your toes on the descent and your torso as upright as possible

When you reach the bottom of the squat, push through your entire foot and drive up from the bottom, ensuring constant tension in your core throughout

Make sure to drive your knees out on the way up and keep your chest up

Pause and repeat

Get into a quadruped position with your hands on the floor under your shoulders, knees under your hips, and toes pointed down to the floor

Posteriorly tilt your pelvis and engage your core, making sure the spine is in a neutral position

Begin the movement by rotating one of your legs out to the side at a 90-degree angle under control

As you move it's important that the movement only comes from externally rotating your hip upwards

Don't rock or lean to the opposite side when you raise your leg as doing so will result in you using momentum to raise your leg and take away from the target muscle working correctly

Make sure your torso stays stationary and your spine neutral throughout

Go as high as your range of motion allows you to, then pause slightly and return to the start position in a slow controlled manner, keeping constant tension in your core and glutes

Lie flat on a decline bench with a dumbbell in each hand

Plant your feet and lift the dumbbells straight above your head. Your spine should be in a neutral position, with your upper back pressed firmly into the bench

Position the dumbbells in a pronated position, palms away from you

Elbows should be shoulder width apart and your arms perpendicular to the floor, elbows soft

Begin the movement by bending your elbows and lowering the dumbbells in a slow controlled manner. Rotate your hands as you lower the dumbbells, so you finish with a neutral grip, palms facing in towards your body

The further behind your head you go with your hands the greater the stretch and difficulty, so find what suits you

Your upper arms should stay perpendicular to the floor and stationery throughout the entire movement

When you can’t go any lower pause and return to the start position in a controlled manner by reversing the movement, keeping constant tension in your triceps

Contract your triceps when you reach the start position and repeat

Lie back on a flat bench with a dumbbell in each hand

Plant your feet and lift the dumbbells straight above your head. Your spine should be in a neutral position, with your upper back pressed firmly into the bench

Position the dumbbells in a neutral position, palms facing in towards your body

Elbows should be shoulder width apart and your arms perpendicular to the floor, elbows soft

Begin the movement by bending your elbows and lowering the dumbbells in a slow controlled manner. The further behind your head you go with your hands the greater the stretch and difficulty, so find what suits you

Your upper arms should stay perpendicular to the floor and stationery throughout the entire movement

When you can’t go any lower pause and return to the start position in a controlled manner, keeping constant tension in your triceps

Contract your triceps when you reach the start position and repeat

Lie flat on a bench with a dumbbell in each hand

Plant your feet and lift the dumbbells straight above your head. Your spine should be in a neutral position, with your upper back pressed firmly into the bench

Position the dumbbells in a pronated position, palms away from you

Elbows should be shoulder width apart and your arms perpendicular to the floor, elbows soft

Begin the movement by bending your elbows and lowering the dumbbells in a slow controlled manner. Rotate your hands as you lower the dumbbells, so you finish with a neutral grip, palms facing in towards your body

The further behind your head you go with your hands the greater the stretch and difficulty, so find what suits you

Your upper arms should stay perpendicular to the floor and stationery throughout the entire movement

When you can’t go any lower pause and return to the start position in a controlled manner by reversing the movement, keeping constant tension in your triceps

Contract your triceps when you reach the start position and repeat

Take up a seated position in front of a cable machine and attach a rope so it’s at chest height

With an upright posture and neutral spine, grab the rope with each hand with an overhand grip

Brace yourself and have your eyes focused looking forward

Begin by bracing your core and retracting your shoulder blades, making sure to keep your shoulders depressed down. Then pull the rope in towards your face in a controlled manner by driving your elbows back

At the same time as your elbows go back, pull the rope apart and bring it towards the top of your head. Hands should finish to the side of your head with your arms near a 90-degree angle. Squeeze your shoulder blades together at this point

Pause and then reverse the movement by returning to the start position in a slow controlled manner, making sure to keep constant tension in your core and upper back while maintaining a neutral spine

Lay on the floor on your back with your feet flat

Head should be neutral or chin tucked (a yoga block or Bosu ball can help prop your head up)

Arms can either be bent by your side with clenched fists or flat by your side

Your hips, shoulders, and knees should be aligned. This is the typical set up for the majority of people

Find a stance that gives the greatest glute activation. You can move your feet closer or further away from your body, have a wider or narrower foot stance, flare your feet in or out, and push your knees out more or less

Start by posteriorly tilting your pelvis and bracing your core, then extend and drive your hips up by pushing through your heels

Knees should track your middle toes throughout. Drive the knees out if needed. Your shins should be roughly vertical to the floor

Make sure to keep your ribs down as this will help prevent spinal extension (lower back curving up) and keep your spine stable

When you reach the end of your range of motion, pause and squeeze your glutes at the top. Return to the start position by reversing the movement in a slow controlled manner, keeping constant tension in the glutes and core

Position a handle attachment at the top of a cable machine and grab with one hand.

Take up a half-kneeling position, with the handle the same side as the downed knee.

Arm should be in a 45-degree angle when the start position is set. Depending on an individual’s mobility around the shoulder, you can decrease or increase the angle to match ability.

From this position, while engaging your core and keeping an upright posture, begin the movement by depressing the shoulder down and retracting the shoulder blade back.

Then pull the handle in towards the body by bringing the elbow down towards the ribs. In this position squeeze your lats and contract.

Ensure the shoulder never elevates up towards the ear during the movement.

Proceed to return the handle back to the start position by reversing the movement in a slow controlled manner, making sure to keep constant tension throughout the lats, core and glutes.

Set up in a quadruped position on your hands and knees, ensuring you have a neutral spine with your hands underneath your shoulders

Your spine should be in a neutral position with your head looking towards the ground

Begin the movement by bracing your core and then extending your leg backward while simultaneously raising the opposite arm in front, finishing with both straight

The aim is to keep your spine neutral and stop any movement occurring in your lower back

Think about pushing your heel backward as you extend your leg, this will help prevent your back from arching

Make a fist with your hand and punch out in front in a controlled manner, initiating a strong contraction in your arm and core muscles as you do so

Pause in this position and then return to the start in a slow controlled manner, ensuring you keep constant tension in your core

Set up an incline bench. Typical angle is 30-degrees but can vary between 15-45 degrees depending on personal preference

Sat at the end of a bench with the barbell behind you, lay back and position yourself so the barbell is in line with your eyes or slightly behind. Be careful not to hit the bar as you lie backwards

Grab the bar slightly wider than shoulder-width apart with both hands and plant your feet into the ground

Next, pinch your shoulder blades together and push your chest out. You should then have a slight arch in your lower back with your upper back pressed against the bench

Proceed to lift the bar from the rack or have a partner help you. The bar should be positioned over the top of your chest, this is your starting position

Start by bracing your core, squeezing your glutes, and pushing your feet into the floor. Then lower the barbell in a slow controlled manner by bending your elbows and bringing the bar towards your chest. Your arms should be at a 45-degree angle to the body at the bottom of the movement

The bar should finish towards the top of your chest, either carefully touching your chest or finishing just short

Proceed to then reverse the movement by driving your feet through the floor and pushing the barbell back up to the starting position, ensuring you keep your shoulder blades retracted and pressed into the bench

Finish just short of locking out your elbows and make sure your shoulders don’t roll forward at any time throughout the movement. Feet shouldn’t move from the floor at any time

Ensure correct starting position and repeat

Carefully lift up or have a partner hand you a kettlebell and grab at the top of the horns with both hands. The kettlebell should be in front of your thighs with your feet hip-width apart

Stand in an upright position with a neutral spine and your knees in line with your toes. Shoulders depressed down and head looking forward

From here, while bracing your core, retract your shoulder blades and engage your lats (imagine crushing two oranges between your armpits)

Begin the movement by taking a big inhale and then hinging at the hips and pulling the kettlebell back between the legs, keeping the knees soft

The upper arms should be against the ribs and the forearms high in the groin

Once the hips are loaded and you have reached the end of your range of motion, reverse the motion by explosively driving your hips forward while pushing down into the ground with the feet

Exhale as you drive your hips forward and contract your glutes at the top

Bring the body to a standing position. Once the kettlebell is less than shoulder height, let it come back down on the same arc it went up

Maintain constant tension through the core, glutes, hamstrings, and upper back

Stand just wider than shoulder-width apart and carefully lift up a kettlebell with one hand

Brace your core and lift the kettlebell above your head. You should have a straight line through your shoulder, elbow, and wrist with the kettlebell resting on the back of your wrist

Begin by bracing your core and hinging at your hips, keeping your knees soft. Your free arm should be straight and as you hinge your hips this hand moves towards the floor in a slow controlled manner

Your torso will slightly rotate as you lower but you should never lose a neutral spine

The arm with the kettlebell should be perpendicular to the floor throughout

Keep your eyes fixed on the kettlebell at all times

Stop when you reach the end of your range of motion and then reverse the movement, making sure to keep constant tension in your core while keeping your eyes fixed on the kettlebell

Sit with your chest against the pad and grab a handle in each hand.

Begin by retracting your shoulder blades, making sure to keep your shoulders depressed down. Then pull the handles in a controlled manner by rowing your elbows back.

Squeeze your shoulder blades together at this point and pause slightly.

Return to the start position by reversing the movement by returning to the start position in a slow controlled manner, making sure to keep constant tension in your upper back.

Set up in a quadruped position on your hands and knees, ensuring you have a neutral spine with your hands underneath your shoulders

Your spine should be in a neutral position with your head looking towards the ground

Place a band under one of your hands and grasp with the other, ensuring enough resistance

Begin the movement by externally rotating the arm with the band so your palm is facing forward and your thumb is facing up. Your arm should be out to the side

With this arm straight, retract your shoulder blade and raise the arm in a controlled manner

Ensure there is no rotation in your torso as you raise the arm and that you keep a neutral spine. As you raise your arm make sure your shoulder doesn't elevate up towards your ear

Pause at the top and then return to the start position in a slow controlled manner, ensuring constant tension in your upper back, shoulder, and core

Ensure your shoulder stays externally rotated at all times

Set up in a quadruped position on your hands and knees, ensuring you have a neutral spine with your hands underneath your shoulders

Your spine should be in a neutral position with your head looking towards the ground

Place a band under one of your hands and grasp with the other, ensuring enough resistance

Begin the movement by externally rotating the arm with the band so your thumb is facing up. Your arm should be positioned at a 45-degree angle, a Y position

With this arm straight, retract your shoulder blade and raise the arm in a controlled manner

Ensure there is no rotation in your torso as you raise the arm and that you keep a neutral spine. As you raise your arm make sure your shoulder doesn't elevate up towards your ear

Pause at the top and then return to the start position in a slow controlled manner, ensuring constant tension in your upper back, shoulder, and core

Ensure your shoulder stays externally rotated at all times

Set up in a quadruped position on your hands and knees, ensuring you have a neutral spine with your hands underneath your shoulders

Your spine should be in a neutral position with your head looking towards the ground

Begin the movement by externally rotating one arm so your palm is facing forward and your thumb is facing up. Your arm should be out to the side

With this arm straight, retract your shoulder blade and raise the arm in a controlled manner

Ensure there is no rotation in your torso as you raise the arm and that you keep a neutral spine. As you raise your arm make sure your shoulder doesn't elevate up towards your ear

Pause at the top and then return to the start position in a slow controlled manner, ensuring constant tension in your upper back, shoulder, and core

Ensure your shoulder stays externally rotated at all times

Set up in a prone position on the floor with both legs at 90-degrees and your heels touching the floor. Place your arms straight by your side

Your spine should be in a neutral position with your head against the floor and your back flat

Begin by posteriorly tilting your pelvis and bracing your core, then bring your knees in towards your chest in a slow controlled manner, making sure to keep your legs at the same angle

As you bring your knees in, raise your hips from the floor while keeping your core engaged and your spine neutral

Pause in this position and contract your abs, then return your hips to the floor and lower your legs back to the start position in a slow controlled manner, ensuring constant tension in your core throughout

Gently touch the floor and then repeat

Lie on your back with the back of your legs on a foam roller, just above the ankle

Place a band around one of your feet and grasp with both hands

Posteriorly tilt your pelvis and brace your core, then raise the leg with the band (keeping it relaxed) as high as possible without the knee bending

Start the movement by engaging your core and raising the other leg to the same level as the assisted leg

Pause at the top and then return to the start position in a slow controlled manner, keeping constant tension in your core as you lower your leg

Ensure you maintain the position of the assisted leg as you lower the other leg. Keep a neutral spine throughout

Gently touch the roller and repeat

Carefully pick up or have a partner hand you a kettlebell in one hand

Lift the kettlebell above your head. You should have a straight line through your shoulder, elbow, and wrist

Feet should be shoulder-width apart with your head facing forward and make sure you have a neutral spine

Begin by bracing your core and keeping a nice upright posture

Walk forward for the allotted distance, making sure you keep your core engaged at all times

Carefully pick up or have a partner hand you a kettlebell in one hand

Lift the kettlebell above your head. You should have a straight line through your shoulder, elbow, and wrist

Feet should be shoulder-width apart with your head facing forward and make sure you have a neutral spine

Begin by bracing your core and keeping a nice upright posture

Maintain this position while keeping constant tension in your core and glutes

Lay on the floor on your back with your feet flat together along your centre line and elevate one leg (can be bent or straight)

Head should be neutral or chin tucked (a yoga block or Bosu ball can help prop your head up)

Carefully lift or have a partner hand you a kettlebell and place on your hips. Hold the dumbbell in place with both hands (You can also hold with one hand while having the other arm by your side)

Start by posteriorly tilting your pelvis and bracing your core, then extend and drive your hips up by pushing through your heel

Make sure to keep your ribs down as this will help prevent spinal extension (lower back curving up) and keep your spine stable

When you reach the end of your range of motion, pause and squeeze your glute at the top. Return to the start position by reversing the movement in a slow controlled manner, keeping constant tension in the glute and core

Start with your feet hip-width apart

Stand in an upright position with a neutral spine and your knees in line with your toes. Shoulders depressed down and head looking forward

From here, while bracing your core, begin the movement by hinging at the hips and lifting one leg off the floor. The arm which is the same side as the moving leg should be straight and moving parallel to the standing leg

Your torso and back leg should move at the same time, with your torso going forward and down and your leg going back and up

Make sure to avoid any bending in the spine when your torso is moving towards the floor

The standing leg should remain stationary with the knee soft, making sure not to lock it out

Lower your torso in a controlled manner until you reach the end of your range of motion

When you reach the end of your range of motion, pause and then return to the start position by driving the hips forward and bringing the moving leg back to the start. Maintain constant tension through the core, glutes, hamstrings and upper back

Bring the foot to the ground after each rep or if ability allows you can keep it off for the duration of the set

Take a shoulder width stance with your feet and grab a dumbbell in one hand

Position it at the top of the shoulder, with the palm facing inwards towards the midline of the body and your elbow tucked in and pointed down

From this position, while engaging your core and glutes, drive the dumbbell above the head

Make sure the dumbbell finishes above the head at shoulder width, with the bicep in line with the ears

Ensure shoulder doesn’t elevate up towards the ear during pressing movement

Proceed to lower the dumbbell back to the start position in a slow controlled manner, making sure to keep constant tension throughout the shoulder, core and glutes

Anchor a band and hold in one hand with your arm straight

Then lie on the side that has no band with your hand flat against the floor. There should be no slack in the band

Your head should be facing forward, and you should have a neutral spine

You can either position your feet on top of each other or split your stance, so your top leg is in front of the other

Start by bracing your core and elevating your hips off the floor until your body is in a straight line and your arm is straight

Maintain this position while keeping constant tension in your core and glutes. The aim is to stop yourself rotating forward with the band

Grasp a pull-up bar with an overhand grip and place them wider than shoulder-width. Stand on a step, bench, etc if needed

Engage your core and ensure you have a neutral spine

If elevated up, begin by carefully stepping forward off the step until you’re hanging with your arms straight and holding your full bodyweight

Either place your legs straight down and feet slightly in front or hook them behind you. Keep a neutral spine for the entire movement

Then depress your shoulder blades down and pull yourself up towards the bar, driving your elbows down in the process

Pull up until your chin clears the bar, keeping your eyes and head level for the entire move

Pause at the top and then return to the start position in a slow controlled manner, ensuring constant tension in your lats, core, and upper back

Stance - About the same stance for a vertical jump, about 8-12 inches between the heels with the toes pointed out (bigger, taller people with wider hips will use a proportionately wider stance)

The bar should be 1-1 ½ inches from your shins, placing the bar directly over the middle of the foot (Having the bar over the mid-foot helps with balance and for an efficient pull)

Ensure you have a neutral spine with your arms straight by your side.

Then take your grip on the bar by bending over at the waist (hip hinging), straight legs but knees soft, without lowering your hips

Follow this by bending your knees and dropping them forward (just to the point where your shins touch the bar), securely gripping the bar. Don't let your hips drop down

It's very important that at points 4 & 5 you do not move the bar. You want to keep it over your mid-foot

Shove your knees out a little, putting them in contact with your knees. Then squeeze your chest up by shoving your rib cage up so your chest rotates up between your arms, staring at a point 12-15 feet in front of you on the floor

Start the pull by taking a big breath (bracing your core) and driving your feet down through the floor, dragging the bar up your legs. Keep the bar in contact with your legs all the way up (keeping the bar path a straight vertical line)

Lift your chest at the top of the pull (don't shrug your shoulders or lean back excessively) and fully extend your hips and knees, standing tall with a neutral spine

Lower the bar by first unlocking your hips and knees, then shove your hips backward and let the bar slide down the thighs in a straight vertical line

As the bar passes your knees, bend them to finish setting the bar down, ensuring constant tension in your core, lats, upper back, glutes, and hamstrings

Once set on the floor, reset and repeat

Position a band under both feet and grasp the ends with your hands with an underhand grip, palms facing away from the body

Stand tall with your arms straight by your side

A neutral spine should be maintained for the entire movement and eyes focused looking forward

Start by bracing your core and then hinging at the hips until your torso is as parallel to the floor as your mobility allows

Then retract your shoulder blades while making sure to keep your shoulders depressed down. Then quickly bring both hands up towards your torso in a controlled manner by rowing your elbows to your side. Squeeze your shoulder blades together at this point

Pulling motion should be initiated from your lats and middle back, rather than just your arms

Pause and then reverse the movement by returning to the start position in a slow controlled manner, making sure to keep constant tension in your core, lats, and upper back while maintaining a neutral spine

Carefully pick up or have a partner hand you a dumbbell in each hand

Start with feet shoulder-width apart and facing forward

Begin by engaging your core and taking a step forward with one leg. While moving the leg in a controlled manner, bend at the knee until you reach a 90-degree angle

Keep on the toes with the back foot

The back leg should also be in a 90-degree angle and should be kept stationary throughout the movement

Be careful not to hit the back knee off the floor with any force and that the forward knee doesn’t track too far over your toes

Make sure to keep a neutral spine with an upright posture, your eyes focused out in front

From here, return to the start position by driving off the front foot and maintaining an upright torso

Repeat on the other side

Lay on the floor on your back with your legs straight

Head should be neutral or chin tucked (a yoga block or Bosu ball can help prop your head up)

Roll a barbell over your legs or have a partner place the bar over your hips. Pad and bar should be centred over your hips

Then bring your feet in so they’re flat together along your centre line and grab the bar wide enough so there’s a slight bend in your elbows

Get into your b-stance by moving one leg forward, placing your heel just in front of the other foot

Start by posteriorly tilting your pelvis and bracing your core, then extend and drive your hips through the bar by pushing through you the heel of the foot closest to your body. Use your arms to push the barbell to keep it in place

The majority of your weight should be on the leg closest to you, the extended leg is purely for balance and stability

Make sure to keep your ribs down as this will help prevent spinal extension (lower back curving up) and keep your spine stable

When you reach the end of your range of motion, pause and squeeze your glute at the top. Return to the start position by reversing the movement in a slow controlled manner, keeping constant tension in the glute and core. Continue pushing the barbell with your arms to keep it on your hips

Secure a bench, box or aerobic step so it won’t slide backwards. Sit in front and position your upper back against the bench, with the bottom of your shoulder blades aligned up with the edge of the bench

Position a band around your knees. Can place above or below, find what position gives you the best glute activation

Bend your arms and clench your fists, while pushing the back of them into the bench

With your feet flat, move them closer or further away so that your shins are roughly vertical when your hips are extended. Find what works for you

Next, position your feet-shoulder width apart and pointed forward. Then straighten one leg, placing your heel just in front of the other foot and point your toes in the air

Your head should be neutral or have your chin tucked. If chin is tucked keep a forward gaze throughout the movement

Start by posteriorly tilting your pelvis and bracing your core, then push your knees out and drive your hips up by pushing through the heel of the foot closest to your body

The majority of your weight should be on the leg closest to you, the extended leg is purely for balance and stability

Knee should track your middle toes. Make sure to keep your ribs down as this will help prevent spinal extension (lower back curving up) and keep your spine stable

When you reach the end of your range of motion, pause and squeeze your glute at the top. Return to the start position by reversing the movement in a slow controlled manner, keeping your upper back and elbows pinned against the bench while keeping constant tension in your glute and core

Anchor a band around one foot and grab the band with the opposite hand. Elevate your other foot behind you

Stand in an upright position with a neutral spine and your knees in line with your toes. Shoulders depressed down and head looking forward

From here, while bracing your core, retract your shoulder blades and engage your lats (imagine crushing two oranges between your armpits)

Begin the movement by hinging at the hips and lowering the band in a controlled manner, ensuring you keep your back straight throughout

Your torso and the band should move at the same time

Make sure to avoid any bending in the spine

The standing leg should remain stationary with the knee soft, making sure not to lock it out

Lower the band in a controlled manner until you reach the end of your range of motion

When you reach the end of your range of motion, pause and then return to the start position by driving the hips forward and pulling the band up. Maintain constant tension through the core, glutes, hamstrings, and upper back

Anchor a band around the middle of a pull-up bar

Stand on a step, bench, etc, and place one foot in the band. Grasp a pull up bar with an overhand grip and place them inside of shoulder-width apart

Engage your core and ensure you have a neutral spine

Begin by carefully stepping forward off the step until you’re hanging with your arms straight and holding your full bodyweight.

Leg with the band should be straight down. Either wrap your free foot around your other foot or bend the leg behind you to 90-degrees. Keep a neutral spine for the entire movement

Then depress your shoulder blades down and pull yourself up towards the bar, driving your elbows down in the process

Pull up until your chin clears the bar, keeping your eyes and head level for the entire move

Pause at the top and then return to the start position in a slow controlled manner, ensuring constant tension in your lats, core, and upper back

Position a band under both feet and grasp the ends with your hands with an overhand grip, palms facing towards the body

Stand tall with your arms straight by your side

A neutral spine should be maintained for the entire movement and eyes focused looking forward

Start by bracing your core and then hinging at the hips until your torso is as parallel to the floor as your mobility allows

Then retract your shoulder blades while making sure to keep your shoulders depressed down. Then quickly bring both hands up towards your torso in a controlled manner by rowing your elbows to your side. Squeeze your shoulder blades together at this point

Pulling motion should be initiated from your lats and middle back, rather than just your arms

Pause and then reverse the movement by returning to the start position in a slow controlled manner, making sure to keep constant tension in your core, lats, and upper back while maintaining a neutral spine

Set up in a quadruped position on your hands and knees, ensuring you have a neutral spine with your hands underneath your shoulders

Your spine should be in a neutral position with your head looking towards the ground

Anchor a band around a secure surface such as a dumbbell and grasp it with one hand

Bring this arm parallel with your torso and then place it at a ninety-degree angle

Start the movement by pushing the band backward and extending your arm, ensuring you keep your upper arm stationary at all times

Stop just short of locking out your elbow and pause slightly, contracting your triceps. Then return to the start position in a slow controlled manner, ensuring constant tension in your triceps and core

Keep a neutral spine throughout, making sure to stop any rotation in your torso

Sat on the floor with both legs straight, anchor a band around one foot, and then grasp the end of the band in the opposite hand with a neutral grip, your palms facing towards the midline of your body

A neutral spine should be maintained for the entire movement and eyes focused looking forward

Start by bracing your core and then retract your shoulder blade while making sure to keep your shoulders depressed down. Then quickly pull the band in towards your torso in a controlled manner by rowing your elbow to your side. Squeeze your shoulder blades together at this point

Pulling motion should be initiated from your lat and middle back, rather than just your arm

Pause and then reverse the movement by returning to the start position in a slow controlled manner, making sure to keep constant tension in your core, lat, and upper back while maintaining a neutral spine

Position a band under one foot and grasp the ends with both hands

Start by getting into a half-kneeling position, both knees at 90-degree angles or slightly smaller. Have the downed knee on a mat or pad

The front foot should be flat while the back foot should have the toes pointed down into the ground

To begin, while bracing your core, drive through the front foot and elevate yourself up, extending both legs in the process

Eyes should be focused looking forward and ensure you keep a neutral spine

From here, begin by bending both knees and descending in a slow and controlled manner until the back knee touches the pad, making sure to keep constant tension throughout the shoulders, core, and glutes

Position a band under one foot and grasp with the same side hand. Place your arm straight by your side with your palms facing forwards

Get into a split stance by stepping forward with the opposite foot and slightly bending your knee. You can use the free hand to brace against your leg

Brace your core and make sure you have a neutral spine. Your arm shouldn’t be locked out

With your arm locked in by your side, begin the movement by bending at the elbow and curling the band up in a controlled fashion

Curl the band as high as you can while maintaining constant tension in your bicep and keeping your upper arm fixed by your side

Be careful not to shrug your shoulders up as you curl the band, and be sure to avoid any swinging to create momentum

Pause when you reach this point and then return to the start position in a slow controlled manner, ensuring constant tension in your bicep and core

Stop just short of locking out your arm and repeat

Start by positioning a band under one foot and grasping the end with the hand on the same side. With the band behind you, bring your hand behind your head

Then split your stance by stepping forward with the opposite foot and slightly bending your knee. Your spine should be in a neutral position, with your core engaged

Place your hand in a neutral position, palm facing in towards the midline of your body

Elbow should be shoulder-width apart. Keep this position for the entire movement, preventing the elbow from flaring out

Begin the movement by pushing the band up with your hand and extending your arm. Don't lock out your elbow at the top

From this position pause slightly and then return to the start position in a slow controlled manner, ensuring constant tension in the triceps and core

Set up a squat rack so the barbell sits at chest height

Grasp the bar just outside shoulder width and step under the middle of the bar with both feet so it’s positioned on the upper back and shoulders, squeezing your shoulder blades together and lifting the elbows up to create a “shelf” for the bar to sit on. This position is dependent on the mobility of the upper body, so find what is comfortable for you

Next, stand up so the barbell comes off the rack and then carefully step far enough back from the rack

Foot position: Start with feet shoulder-width apart and slightly turned out. Some people will prefer a wider stance, or one with the feet turned out even more or with the toes pointed forward. Find what’s comfortable

Once foot position is set, screw the feet into the floor. This will help you create torque as well as grip the floor. Your bodyweight should be centred over the middle of your foot to ensure balance

Stand in an upright position with a neutral spine, with your knees in line with your toes

Start the movement by bracing your core and then bending simultaneously at both the hips and knees, slowly lowering yourself in a controlled manner to the end of your range of motion. A good marker is your thigh parallel to the ground. Everyone is different here. Find a depth that allows you to maintain correct form and a neutral spine

Keep your knees in line with your toes on the descent and your torso as upright as possible

When you reach the bottom of the squat, push through your entire foot and drive up from the bottom, ensuring constant tension in your core throughout

Make sure to drive your knees out on the way up and keep your chest up

The barbell should travel up and down over the middle of the foot in a vertical line

Pause and repeat

Sat at the end of a bench with the barbell behind you, lay back and position yourself so the barbell is in line with your eyes. Be careful not to hit the bar as you lie backwards

Grab the bar slightly wider than shoulder-width apart with both hands. Your legs should be at not more than a 90-degree angle with your feet flat

Next, pinch your shoulder blades together and push your chest out. You should then have a slight arch in your lower back with your upper back pressed against the bench

Proceed to lift the bar from the rack or have a partner help you. The bar should be positioned over the middle of your chest, this is your starting position

Start by bracing your core, squeezing your glutes, and pushing your feet into the floor. Then lower the barbell in a slow controlled manner by bending your elbows and bringing the bar towards your chest. Your arms should be at a 45-degree angle to the body at the bottom of the movement

The bar should finish at the base of your sternum (breastbone), either carefully touching your chest or finishing just short

Proceed to then reverse the movement by driving your feet through the floor and pushing the barbell back up to the starting position, ensuring you keep your shoulder blades retracted and pressed into the bench

Finish just short of locking out your elbows and make sure your shoulders don’t roll forward at any time throughout the movement. Feet shouldn’t move from the floor at any time

Ensure correct starting position and repeat

Set up a squat rack so the barbell sits at chest height

Grasp the bar shoulder width and step towards the middle of the bar with both feet so it’s positioned on the front of your shoulders/collarbones. Push your elbows up from here, with your upper arm parallel to the ground. This position is dependent on the mobility of the upper body, so find what is comfortable for you

Next, stand up so the barbell comes off the rack and then carefully step far enough back from the rack

Foot position: Start with feet shoulder-width apart and slightly turned out. Some people will prefer a wider stance, or one with the feet turned out even more or with the toes pointed forward. Find what’s comfortable

Once the foot position is set, screw the feet into the floor. This will help you create torque as well as grip the floor. Your bodyweight should be centered over the middle of your foot to ensure balance

Stand in an upright position with a neutral spine, with your knees in line with your toes

Start the movement by bracing your core and then bending simultaneously at both the hips and knees, slowly lowering yourself in a controlled manner to the end of your range of motion. A good marker is your thigh parallel to the ground. Everyone is different here. Find a depth that allows you to maintain the correct form and a neutral spine

Keep your knees in line with your toes on the descent and your torso as upright as possible, making sure to keep your elbows up

When you reach the bottom of the squat, push through your entire foot and drive up from the bottom, ensuring constant tension in your core throughout

Make sure to drive your knees out on the way up and keep your chest up

The barbell should travel up and down over the middle of the foot in a vertical line

Pause and repeat

Lay on the floor on your back with your legs straight

Position a band around your knees. Can place above or below, find what position gives you the best glute activation

Head should be neutral or chin tucked (a yoga block or Bosu ball can help prop your head up)

Roll a barbell over your legs or have a partner place the bar over your hips. Pad and bar should be centred over your hips

Grab the bar wide enough so there’s a slight bend in your elbows and then bring your feet in

Your hips, shoulders, and knees should be aligned. This is the typical set up for the majority of people

Find a stance that gives the greatest glute activation. You can move your feet closer or further away from your body, have a wider or narrower foot stance, flare your feet in or out, and push your knees out more or less

Start by posteriorly tilting your pelvis and bracing your core, then extend and drive your hips through the bar and push through your heels. Use your arms to push the barbell to keep it in place

Drive the knees out against the band. Knees should track your middle toes throughout, and your shins should be roughly vertical to the floor

Make sure to keep your ribs down as this will help prevent spinal extension (lower back curving up) and keep your spine stable

When you reach the end of your range of motion, pause and squeeze your glutes at the top. Return to the start position by reversing the movement in a slow controlled manner, continuing to push your knees out against the band while keeping constant tension in the glutes and core. Continue pushing the barbell with your arms to keep it on your hips

Secure a bench, box or aerobic step so it won’t slide backwards. Sit in front and position your upper back and elbows against the bench, with the bottom of your shoulder blades aligned up with the edge of the bench

Position a band around your knees. Can place above or below, find what position gives you the best glute activation

Next, while seated, roll a padded barbell over your legs and place over your hips. To get into your start position you may have to sit on a pad if the bench is too high or slightly lift the bar to get your feet into position. Grab the bar wide enough so there’s a slight bend in your elbows

With your feet flat bring your feet towards your bum. Move them closer or further away so that your shins are roughly vertical when your hips are extended. Find what works for you

Next, position your feet-shoulder width apart and pointed forward. Foot position can vary in people, so find what works for you. You can turn your feet out and have a wider stance for instance

Your head should be neutral or have your chin tucked. If chin is tucked keep a forward gaze throughout the movement

Start by posteriorly tilting your pelvis and bracing your core, then push your knees out against the band and drive your hips through the bar by pushing through your heels. Think of using your glutes to push your hips rather than your spine

Knees should track your middle toes. Make sure to keep your ribs down as this will help prevent spinal extension (lower back curving up) and keep your spine stable

When you reach the end of your range of motion, pause and squeeze your glutes at the top. Return to the start position by reversing the movement in a slow controlled manner, keeping your upper back and elbows pinned against the bench while keeping constant tension in your glutes and core. Continue pushing the barbell with your arms to keep it on your hips

Adjust the rack position so the barbell is just below shoulder height

Take a shoulder width stance with your feet and grab the bar just outside of shoulder width

Position the bar just below the collarbones with your elbows tucked in and pointed down

Carefully step back from the rack and plant your feet in the ground

From this position, while engaging your core and glutes, press the bar to lockout overhead by extending your legs

Make sure the barbell finishes directly above the head, with the biceps in line with the ears

Ensure shoulders don’t elevate up towards the ear during pressing movement

Proceed to lower the bar back to the start position in a slow controlled manner, making sure to keep constant tension throughout the shoulders, core and glutes

Position a barbell so its chest height in a rack or have a partner carefully lift the barbell on your back

Grasp the bar just outside shoulder width and step under the middle of the bar with both feet so it’s positioned on the upper back and shoulders, squeezing your shoulder blades together and lifting the elbows up to create a “shelf” for the bar to sit on. This position is dependent on the mobility of the upper body, so find what is comfortable for you

Next, stand up so the barbell comes off the rack and then carefully step far enough back from the rack

Start with feet shoulder-width apart and facing forward

Begin by engaging your core and taking a step back with one leg. While moving the leg back in a controlled manner, bend at the knee until you reach a 90-degree angle

Keep on the toes for the back foot

The front leg should also be in a 90-degree angle and should be kept stationary and flat throughout the movement

Be careful not to hit the knee off the floor with any force

Make sure to keep a neutral spine with an upright posture, your eyes focused out in front

From here, return to the start position by driving off the back foot and maintaining an upright torso

Sit on a side of a flat bench or box

Place your hands just wider than shoulder-width apart on the edge

Lift yourself off by taking a step forward with both feet and straightening your legs

Your arms should be straight but not locked out, keeping your elbows soft. Retract your shoulder blades and keep your shoulders depressed down

Begin the movement by bending your elbows and lowering yourself in a slow controlled manner, keeping your torso close to the bench as you move and maintaining constant tension in your triceps

Your forearms should stay as perpendicular to the floor as possible, with the majority of the movement coming from the elbow

Lower until your arms reach a 90-degree angle (going slightly lower if able). Make sure your shoulders don't shrug up towards your ears

Pause at the bottom and then reverse the movement by driving through your hands and extending your arms, contracting your triceps when you reach the top

Keep the weight on your hands and not your legs as you move, rather using them to push you yourself up and not your legs

Start in a quadruped position with your hands on the floor under your shoulders, knees under your hips, and toes pointed towards you into the floor.

Position a raised surface behind you such as a step or box (difficulty increases with height) and extend one leg back at a time, so they are positioned atop. Assume a push up position with the legs straight, arms straight, and head in a neutral position looking at the floor

Grip the floor by twisting your hands outwards while keeping them in place.

Pull your shoulder blades into your back pockets.

Extend one leg back at a time with the legs straight, arms straight, and head in a neutral position looking at the floor.

With your core and glutes engaged, and ensuring your back is straight, slowly lower yourself to the floor by bending at the elbows.

Descend until the upper arms are parallel with the floor.

From this position drive your hands through the floor and go back up to the starting point. Keep constant tension in your chest, core and glutes.

Place your hands under your shoulders on an elevated surface like a box or step (the higher the elevation, the easier the difficulty is), with your legs straight and toes pointed down into the floor

Start in a quadruped position with your hands on the floor under your shoulders, knees under your hips, and toes pointed towards you into the floor.

Grip the floor by twisting your hands outwards while keeping them in place.

Pull your shoulder blades into your back pockets.

Extend one leg back at a time with the legs straight, arms straight, and head in a neutral position looking at the floor.

With your core and glutes engaged, and ensuring your back is straight, slowly lower yourself to the floor by bending at the elbows.

Descend until the upper arms are parallel with the step.

From this position drive your hands through the floor and go back up to the starting point. Keep constant tension in your chest, core and glutes.

Start in a quadruped position with your hands on the floor under your shoulders, knees under your hips, and toes pointed down to the floor

Place one of your hands on top of a medicine ball

Extend one leg back at a time and assume a push up position with the legs straight, arm straight on the floor, and head in a neutral position looking at the floor

Grip the floor and ball by twisting your hands in place

With your core and glutes engaged, and ensuring your back is straight, slowly lower yourself by retracting the shoulder blades and bending at the elbows

Descend as low as possible, while keeping the correct position throughout

From this position drive your body back up to the starting point while keeping constant tension throughout the chest, core, and glutes

Start in a quadruped position with your hands on the floor under your shoulders, knees under your hips, and toes pointed towards you into the floor.

Grip the floor by twisting your hands outwards while keeping them in place.

Pull your shoulder blades into your back pockets.

Extend one leg back at a time with the legs straight, arms straight, and head in a neutral position looking at the floor.

With your core and glutes engaged, and ensuring your back is straight, slowly lower yourself to the floor by bending at the elbows.

Descend until the upper arms are parallel with the floor.

From this position drive your hands through the floor and go back up to the starting point. Keep constant tension in your chest, core and glutes.

Angle a box so the corner is positioned between your feet. The box height should allow you to reach somewhat close if not all the way to the end of your range of motion when you squat. Can also use a bench

Foot position: Start with feet shoulder-width apart and slightly turned out. Some people will prefer a wider stance, or one with the feet turned out even more or with the toes pointed forward. Find what’s comfortable

Carefully pick up or have a partner hand you a dumbbell. Hold it at chest height with one hand under each edge of the dumbbell. The dumbbell should be kept close and tight to the body for the entire movement

Once the foot position is set and the dumbbell is in position, screw the feet into the floor. This will help you create torque as well as grip the floor. Your bodyweight should be centered over the middle of your foot to ensure balance

Stand in an upright position with a neutral spine, with your knees in line with your toes

Start the movement by bracing your core and then bending simultaneously at both the hips and knees, slowly lowering yourself in a controlled manner until you sit on the box. Be careful not to descend too quickly so you hit the box with force

Once sat, relax the leg and hip muscles and pause for one or two seconds. It’s important that you keep your spine stable by keeping a rigid torso

Stand up by pushing through your entire foot and driving up from the bottom, ensuring constant tension in your core throughout

Make sure to drive your knees out on the way up and keep your chest up

Pause and repeat

Start by getting into a half-kneeling position, both knees at 90-degree angles or slightly smaller. Have the downed knee on a mat or pad

The front foot should be flat while the back foot should have the toes pointed down into the ground

Pick up a dumbbell in each hand with arms straight by your sides

To begin, while bracing your core, drive through the front foot and elevate yourself up, extending both legs in the process

Eyes should be focused looking forward and ensure you keep a neutral spine

From here, begin by bending both knees and descending in a slow and controlled manner until the back knee touches the pad, making sure to keep constant tension throughout the shoulders, core and glutes

Get into a quadruped position on your hands and knees with two dumbbells

Make sure you have a neutral spine and then brace your core

Get into the start position by straitening both legs. Arms should be straight, eyes looking down and core braced

Begin by retracting your shoulder blade while making sure to keep your shoulders depressed down. Then quickly pull the dumbbell up towards your torso in a controlled manner by rowing your elbow to your side. Squeeze your shoulder blades together at this point

Pulling motion should be initiated from your lats and middle back, rather than just your arms

Movement through the body should be limited to the moving arm, hips and spine staying as stationary as possible

Pause and then reverse the movement by returning the dumbbell to the floor in a slow controlled manner, making sure to keep constant tension in your core, lats and upper back while maintaining a neutral spine

Start with feet shoulder-width apart and facing forward

Carefully pick up or have a partner hand you a dumbbell in each hand

Begin by engaging your core and taking a step back with one leg. While moving the leg back in a controlled manner, bend at the knee until you reach a 90-degree angle

Keep on the toes for the back foot

The front leg should also be in a 90-degree angle and should be kept stationary and flat throughout the movement

Be careful not to hit the knee off the floor with any force

Make sure to keep a neutral spine with an upright posture, your eyes focused out in front

From here, return to the start position by driving off the back foot and maintaining an upright torso

Set up a bench at a 75–90-degree angle and sit (can also perform this exercise with no back support)

Place your arms straight by your side with a dumbbell in each hand. Take up a neutral grip, palms facing in towards your body

Brace your core and make sure you have a neutral spine. Your arms shouldn’t be locked out

With your arms locked in by your side, begin the movement by bending one of your elbows and curling the dumbbell up in a controlled fashion

Curl it as high as you can while maintaining constant tension on your bicep and keeping your upper arm fixed by your side

Be careful not to shrug your shoulders up as you curl the dumbbell, and be sure to avoid any swinging to create momentum

Pause when you reach this point and then return to the start position in a slow controlled manner, ensuring constant tension on your bicep and core

Stop just short of locking out your arm and repeat on the other side

Stand on a plate(s) or a small platform

Stance - About the same stance for a vertical jump, about 8-12 inches between the heels with the toes pointed out (bigger, taller people with wider hips will use a proportionately wider stance)

The bar should be 1-1 ½ inches from your shins, placing the bar directly over the middle of the foot (Having the bar over the mid-foot helps with balance and for an efficient pull)

Ensure you have a neutral spine with your arms straight by your side.

Then take your grip on the bar by bending over at the waist (hip hinging), straight legs but knees soft, without lowering your hips

Follow this by bending your knees and dropping them forward (just to the point where your shins touch the bar), securely gripping the bar. Don't let your hips drop down

It's very important that at points 4 & 5 you do not move the bar. You want to keep it over your mid-foot

Shove your knees out a little, putting them in contact with your knees. Then squeeze your chest up by shoving your rib cage up so your chest rotates up between your arms, staring at a point 12-15 feet in front of you on the floor

Start the pull by taking a big breath (bracing your core) and driving your feet down through the floor, dragging the bar up your legs. Keep the bar in contact with your legs all the way up (keeping the bar path a straight vertical line)

Lift your chest at the top of the pull (don't shrug your shoulders or lean back excessively) and fully extend your hips and knees, standing tall with a neutral spine

Lower the bar by first unlocking your hips and knees, then shove your hips backward and let the bar slide down the thighs in a straight vertical line

As the bar passes your knees, bend them to finish setting the bar down, ensuring constant tension in your core, lats, upper back, glutes, and hamstrings

Once set on the floor, reset and repeat

Stance - About the same stance for a vertical jump, about 8-12 inches between the heels with the toes pointed out (bigger, taller people with wider hips will use a proportionately wider stance)

Stand in the middle of the trap bar, with the middle of your foot in line with the center of the handles (this helps with balance)

Ensure you have a neutral spine with your arms straight by your side

Then take your grip on the bar by bending over at the waist (hip hinging), straight legs but knees soft, without lowering your hips

Follow this by bending your knees and dropping them forward (just to the point where your shins touch the bar), securely gripping the bar. Don't let your hips drop down

It's very important that at points 4 & 5 you do not move the bar. You want to keep it over your mid-foot

Shove your knees out a little,then squeeze your chest up by shoving your rib cage up so your chest rotates up between your arms, staring at a point 12-15 feet in front of you on the floor

Start the pull by taking a big breath (bracing your core) and driving your feet down through the floor, lifting the bar off the floor. Keep the bar path in as straight a vertical line as possible)

Lift your chest at the top of the pull (don't shrug your shoulders or lean back excessively) and fully extend your hips and knees, standing tall with a neutral spine

Lower the bar by first unlocking your hips and knees, then shove your hips backward and let the bar travel down in a straight vertical line

As the bar passes your knees, bend them to finish setting the bar down, ensuring constant tension in your core, lats, upper back, glutes, and hamstrings

Once set on the floor, reset and repeat

Position a handle about two feet from the floor on a cable machine. Then lie on your side with your forearm against the floor and fist clenched.

Your head should be facing forward, and you should have a neutral spine

You can either position your feet on top of each other or split your stance, so your top leg is in front of the other

Grab the handle with your hand and straighten your arm out in front

Start by bracing your core and elevating your hips off the floor until your body is in a straight line. Maintain this position while keeping constant tension in your core and glutes

Next, retract your shoulder blade and then quickly pull the handle in a controlled manner by rowing your elbow to your side so your hand finishes beside your chest. Squeeze your shoulder blades together at this point

Pause and then reverse the movement by returning to the start position in a slow controlled manner, making sure to keep constant tension in your core and upper back while maintaining a neutral spine

Lie on your back with your head propped up on a Bosu ball and your heels together

Position a band around your knees. Can place above or below, find what position gives you the best glute activation

Head should be neutral or chin tucked (a yoga block or Bosu ball can help prop your head up)

Arms can either be bent by your side with clenched fists or flat by your side

With stance and knee position, you want to find a position where your feet are not too close or too far from the body, and where your knees are not too straight in the air or touching the floor. Find the spot in the middle that gives you the best glute activation

Start by posteriorly tilting your pelvis and bracing your core, then extend and drive your hips up by pushing through the outside of your heels

Feet and knees should stay in the same position throughout, making sure to push out against the band at all times

Make sure to keep your ribs down as this will help prevent spinal extension (lower back curving up) and keep your spine stable

When you reach the end of your range of motion, pause and squeeze your glutes at the top. Return to the start position by reversing the movement in a slow controlled manner, keeping constant tension in the glutes and core

Grasp a chin-up bar with a shoulder-width apart position so your palms are facing you. Stand on a step, bench, etc if needed

Engage your core and ensure you have a neutral spine

If elevated up, begin by carefully stepping forward off the step until you’re hanging with your arms straight and holding your full bodyweight

Either place your legs straight down and feet slightly in front or hook them behind you. Keep a neutral spine for the entire movement

Then depress your shoulder blades down and pull yourself up towards the bar, driving your elbows down in the process

Pull up until your chin clears the bar, keeping your eyes and head level for the entire move

Pause at the top and then return to the start position in a slow controlled manner, ensuring constant tension in your lats, core, and upper back

Set up on a decline bench and carefully pick a dumbbell up in each hand.

Retract the shoulder blades, then carefully lie back and push the dumbbells up into the air so the arms are straight above you and perpendicular to the floor, with the dumbbells at a 45-degree angle.

Begin by slowly lowering one of the dumbbells until the handle is level with the outside of the chest and the arm is at a 45-degree angle to the body.

Proceed to then push your feet through the floor and drive the dumbbell back up to the starting position by pushing it up and bringing the arm in towards the midline of the body, contracting the chest at the top.

Make sure to keep constant tension throughout the chest and core.

Repeat with the other side.

Sat at the end of a decline bench with the barbell behind you, lay back and position yourself so the barbell is in line with your eyes or slightly behind. Be careful not to hit the bar as you lie backwards

Your feet should be firmly planted and if possible, your legs should be secured with a pad

Grab the bar slightly wider than shoulder-width apart with both hands and plant your feet into the ground

Proceed to lift the bar from the rack or have a partner help you. The bar should be positioned over the middle of your chest, this is your starting position

Start by bracing your core, squeezing your glutes, and pushing your feet into the floor. Then lower the barbell in a slow controlled manner by bending your elbows and bringing the bar towards your chest. Your arms should be at a 45-degree angle to the body at the bottom of the movement

The bar should finish at the base of your sternum (breastbone), either carefully touching your chest or finishing just short

Proceed to then reverse the movement by driving your feet through the floor and pushing the barbell back up to the starting position, ensuring you keep your shoulder blades retracted and pressed into the bench

Finish just short of locking out your elbows and make sure your shoulders don’t roll forward at any time throughout the movement. Feet shouldn’t move from the floor at any time

Ensure correct starting position and repeat

Lie flat on a decline bench with a dumbbell in each hand

Plant your feet and lift the dumbbells straight above your head. Your spine should be in a neutral position, with your upper back pressed firmly into the bench

Position the dumbbells in a neutral position, palms facing in towards your body

Elbows should be shoulder width apart and your arms perpendicular to the floor, elbows soft

Begin the movement by bending your elbows and lowering the dumbbells in a slow controlled manner. The further behind your head you go with your hands the greater the stretch and difficulty, so find what suits you

Your upper arms should stay perpendicular to the floor and stationery throughout the entire movement

When you can’t go any lower pause and return to the start position in a controlled manner, keeping constant tension in your triceps

Contract your triceps when you reach the start position and repeat

Carefully lift up or have a partner hand you a kettlebell in each hand, grabbing them at the top of the horns. The kettlebells should be in front of your thighs with your feet slightly wider than shoulder-width apart

Stand in an upright position with a neutral spine and your knees in line with your toes. Shoulders depressed down and head looking forward

From here, while bracing your core, retract your shoulder blades and engage your lats (imagine crushing two oranges between your armpits)

Begin the movement by taking a big inhale and then hinging at the hips and pulling the kettlebells back between the legs, keeping the knees soft

The upper arms should be against the ribs and the forearms high in the groin

Once the hips are loaded and you have reached the end of your range of motion, reverse the motion by explosively driving your hips forward while pushing down into the ground with the feet

Exhale as you drive your hips forward and contract your glutes at the top

Bring the body to a standing position. Once the kettlebells are less than shoulder height, let them come back down on the same arc they went up

Maintain constant tension through the core, glutes, hamstrings, and upper back
